notes.note: C. Vergilius, a co-owner whose portion of a riverside property near Rome Cicero hoped to buy. The purchase belongs to Cicero's long hunt for a site for the shrine (fanum) he meant to raise to his dead daughter Tullia.
Clodia, owner of gardens that were Cicero's fallback choice if the Vergilius share fell through. The fanum project drove this anxious comparison of properties.
M. Livius Drusus Claudianus, owner of yet another set of gardens; Cicero fears he may be driven to bid recklessly for them. The 'thing you know of' is the unnamed shrine for Tullia.
Cicero's secluded coastal villa on a small island at the mouth of the river Astura in Latium, where he withdrew to grieve for Tullia in 45 BC.
A letter of advice (the 'letter of counsel') Cicero had drafted for Caesar at Atticus's urging; he repeatedly weighs whether to send it. The doubts continue in letters 13.27 and 13.28.
